title: Incursa.Quic Public Loopback Connection Comparison Architecture
purpose: Describe the narrowest honest side-by-side public-facade connection benchmark boundary against `System.Net.Quic` without conflating internal helper measurements with public connection or stream workloads.
design_summary: The comparison harness stays at one abstraction level for both stacks: it uses only the public listener and connection facades exposed by Incursa.Quic and `System.Net.Quic`. The measured workload set is intentionally small and loopback-only: listener creation, connection accept and connect, and connection disposal on the same machine. The harness creates matching TLS certificate and client trust inputs for both stacks, binds the server to a fresh loopback endpoint for each invocation, and filters benchmark cases through each stack's public support markers (`QuicConnection.IsSupported` and `QuicListener.IsSupported`) so unsupported environments are omitted instead of receiving synthetic numbers.
alternatives_considered: Compare the existing internal congestion-control, RTT, packet, or stream-state helper suites directly against `System.Net.Quic`: rejected because those suites do not operate at the same public abstraction level as `QuicConnection` and `QuicStream`.

Promote a public request/response or stream-throughput comparison in the same slice: rejected for now because the repo does not yet have a separately traced and proven public stream-transfer workload that both stacks can execute honestly.

Use interop-runner or HTTP/3 application workloads as the comparison boundary: rejected because the supported interop surface and HTTP/3 semantics remain intentionally narrower and would mix transport/runtime progress with external harness behavior.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Both stacks must receive matching certificate material, trust anchors, and loopback endpoints so the benchmark does not accidentally compare different TLS or transport setup costs.
Benchmark cases for a given stack are emitted only when that stack's own `IsSupported` marker is true on the current machine.
Each benchmark invocation creates and disposes its own listener and connection state so one iteration cannot reuse or leak transport state into the next.
data_and_state_considerations: The benchmark harness owns only local measurement state: a self-signed loopback certificate, a fresh trust-store copy for the client, and per-invocation loopback endpoints. It must not read or mutate internal transport state from either stack when producing the side-by-side measurements.
risks: If the harness accidentally uses Incursa internal-only helpers or state, the comparison will overstate public parity.
If one stack is allowed to run with a different trust or certificate path, the measured costs will no longer be comparable.
If unsupported environments are allowed to fall through to no-op execution, the exported reports will mislead reviewers.
If later stream-transfer comparison work is folded into this slice without a new requirement and proof plan, reviewers could mistake a connection-only benchmark for a fuller public throughput claim.
